data.title: a16z Deep Dive into Revolut: A Growth Engine for Continuous Compound Interest
data.description:
Source: a16z; Compiled by: Jinse Finance

As growth investors, we often say that great companies begin with their financial data. Take Revolut as an example. As a British company, they are required to publish annual financial data—and their data, to put it mildly, is outstanding:

Revenue grew by 46% to £4.5 billion.

Pre-tax profit increased by 57% to £1.7 billion, with a profit margin of 38%.

Retail customer growth was 30%, adding 16 million new customers in 2025 alone.

Revolut has a high market penetration in Europe, with no country having a fee income ratio exceeding 25%.

Revolut has a high penetration rate in the European market, with no other country having a fee income ratio exceeding 25%.

Similarly, revenue is currently distributed across six business segments, with no single segment accounting for more than 22% of total revenue. Eleven different product lines generate over £100 million in revenue. Despite its high capital adequacy ratio, its return on equity (ROE) is a remarkable 35%, far exceeding that of its peers. Revolut continues its rapid and efficient growth – its 75% growth rate (defined as revenue growth + net profit margin) is among the highest in modern, established financial institutions. More importantly, we believe Revolut still has significant room for growth in its existing markets, both in terms of user growth and profitability. Not to mention the potential, readily available new markets – Revolut has just applied for a US license and has truly global ambitions. The key point is: this isn't the kind of new-age bank your grandmother's generation remembers. Revolut has the potential to become one of the world's largest banks. While there's still a lot of work to be done to achieve this, we believe the foundation is laid. Let's begin. Let's look at the revenue figures first. Revolut's revenue growth is phenomenal. Along with NU (Nubank), Revolut stands out in the consumer fintech industry (as shown in the chart below). Since surpassing $1 billion in revenue in 2022, Revolut has achieved an astonishing 76% CAGR (or 70% in pounds sterling) over the following four years, making it one of the fastest-growing companies after reaching $1 billion in revenue. This growth is particularly noteworthy considering Europe's highly developed consumer banking sector (unlike the less developed markets where NU operates).

Source: Revolut 2025 Annual Report, a16z Analysis. Revenue is converted to USD at year-end exchange rate. NU revenue has deducted interest and expected credit losses.

In other words, in 2022, Revolut's revenue was lower than (or equal to) any of Robinhood, Affirm, SoFi, Adyen, Wise, or Chime. Now, its revenue is 33% to nearly three times higher than these well-known consumer fintech companies.

II. AnalysisRevolutGrowth Algorithm: Six Winning Strategies (and More)

A key advantage of Revolut is that it no longer has a single source of revenue. It has multiple revenue drivers, and these factors are all working simultaneously.

Revolut was initially designed to address a pain point for Europeans: foreign exchange fees. With Revolut, Europeans traveling to and from the Eurozone or sending money abroad no longer need to face payment delays or the 5% fee charged by banks.

What was once a single-product, geographically focused, and optional service provider has evolved into a full-fledged commercial and personal bank. In Europe (Revolut's current main operating region), Revolut accounts for approximately one-third of new account openings: [Image of Revolut profile: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7442044_watermarknone.png](https://img.jinse.com.cn/7442044_watermarknone.png) [Image of Revolut profile: uuEsH29uNpGjbdmbiQH62u7moIlKHhTjZW2j0vzy.png) ... Respondents are required to specify the institution where their accounts are held and the opening date of each account. In Europe, one in five working-age adults now use Revolut. Revolut's popularity across Europe reflects the company's impressive product speed and execution. Revolut offers a complete suite of personal and corporate banking services, driving its growth in various European markets. More importantly, Revolut's product suite is increasingly appealing to Eurozone users who may not care about its initial forex trading value proposition. We would have liked to call Revolut's platform "feature-rich," but Revolut continues to introduce new features, so that assessment may not be sufficient. Revolut's strength lies not only in the number of new features and products it offers, but also in the quality of its execution. Customers rave about it. A report released by the company in 2024 showed that 65% of new customers came through organic growth or referrals from existing customers. Our research also shows that Revolut's Net Promoter Score (NPS) is more than double the industry average.¹ Taking all factors into account, the end result is that the number of users continues to grow at a compound annual growth rate of 30%, reaching 68 million by the end of 2025. Source: Revolut Annual Report. To better understand these 68 million users, we can refer to the case of JPMorgan Chase (the world's largest bank outside of China): JPMorgan Chase has approximately 85 million consumer customers (of which over 70 million are considered "digitally active users"). It's worth noting that JPMorgan Chase's total assets under management are orders of magnitude larger than Revolut's, but in terms of user reach, Revolut is no longer just a "challenger"; it has become a true industry giant. Revolut's user base exceeds the combined total of SoFi, Robinhood, Dave, and Chime. A complete product portfolio not only continues to attract more customers but also creates an increasingly diversified revenue structure: Source: Revolut Annual Report. The company's six publicly disclosed main revenue streams are: interest income; debit card payments; wealth management; FX; subscriptions; and other revenue. All six business segments achieved year-over-year growth, and no single business segment accounted for more than 22% of revenue. The company's business diversification extends far beyond the figures mentioned above, as each revenue stream may encompass multiple sub-products (for example, its wealth management business covers public equities and cryptocurrencies). By 2025, it will have 11 different product lines generating over £100 million in revenue. Notably, 76% of its revenue comes from fees, an increase of over 4 percentage points from 2024, while interest income accounts for less than 22%. This contrasts sharply with traditional banks where over 70% of revenue comes from interest, which is one reason why Revolut achieves a high return on equity (more on this below). Unsurprisingly, this diversified revenue base translates into diversified ARPU growth.

Source: Revolut Annual Report, a16z Analysis. ARPU is defined as revenue per product line / average revenue per user during the period.

Since 2022, all reported revenue sources have grown, with total ARPU increasing by approximately 65%, or a compound annual growth rate of 18%.

Diversification is crucial because it helps to sustain compound growth and enhance resilience. Any year can see explosive growth in a product line, or it can face adversity (such as last year's declining interest rates).

Overall, however, the addition of new products and the continued growth in market share of core products still drive strong growth in average revenue per user (ARPU). Revolut has demonstrated rapid user growth, extremely fast product iteration, and diversified revenue streams, but we are also committed to improving efficiency. In 2025, Revolut achieved 46% revenue growth and a 29% net profit margin, resulting in an X-rule (growth rate + profit margin) of 75%. Now the 40 rule no longer applies!

Data source: Publicly available financial data provided by CapIQ, a16z analysis.

For companies that have not yet released financial reports, the data is 2025 A data or current analyst forecasts. Bubble size represents total revenue in 2025. NU revenue has deducted interest and expected credit losses.

This company combines growth and efficiency, placing it among the top in the industry—only a very few companies have achieved the 75% rule with revenue exceeding $1 billion.

In fact, considering that Robinhood and Dave are projected to grow by less than 30% next year (according to consensus), Revolut may soon take the lead. Efficiency is in Revolut's DNA. The company prioritizes developing its own banking infrastructure, drives highly organic growth, and rigorously controls costs, resulting in a net profit margin of 29%. Coupled with a limited number of physical branches, Revolut currently has a significant cost advantage over traditional banks, an advantage that will continue to strengthen as the company grows. Finally, the company also demonstrated how artificial intelligence can further improve operational efficiency. For example, in customer service: In 2024, Revolut's chatbot assistant reduced problem-solving time by 80%. In 2025, this improvement continued, with retail customer issue resolution times reduced by over 40% and enterprise customer issue resolution times reduced by over 50%—while Net Promoter Score (NPS) increased by nearly 12 percentage points year-over-year. Revolut's assistants are now able to resolve over 75% of customer inquiries. All these efficiency gains have enabled Revolut to achieve the highest scalable ROE we've seen in the fintech space (and it continues to improve). We've previously discussed the importance of ROE for bank valuations, and Revolut is a prime example of scalable, efficient operations. Source: Public financial data provided by CapIQ, analyzed by a16z. Return on equity (ROE) is defined as net profit in 2025 divided by average equity during that period. Revolut's ROE is as high as 35%, far exceeding other leading consumer fintech companies and approximately 3-4 times that of mature banks. It should be noted that Revolut has a high capital adequacy ratio (i.e., its published equity is higher than the capital adequacy ratio requirements of banks), which means that its "true" ROE may be even higher. It's hard to imagine growth efficiency reaching this level. IV. Ample Room for Growth: ARPU x Number of Users While Revolut's performance in 2025 was impressive, we believe it still has significant room for growth. Looking back at the company's core revenue growth algorithm (number of users x ARPU), both of these growth drivers have considerable room for improvement. The company reports that it will reach 68 million users by the end of 2025. As mentioned above, this is substantial, but it represents less than 15% of the approximately 450-500 million adult population in Europe (excluding Russia). Furthermore, this does not include the growth potential in Australia and Singapore (existing markets), Mexico and Brazil (recent entrants), the United States (recently applied for a banking license), and many more regions in the future. The key is that Revolut still has many more potential users to attract. Furthermore, the current user demographics suggest that the future is unlikely to be the same. Unsurprisingly, Revolut's user base is young and digitally savvy—we believe these customers represent the ultimate needs of most people. Source: a16z European Banking Survey, February 2026 (N=4,200). The survey markets include the UK, Ireland, France, Spain, Italy, Germany, and Poland. As Revolut continues to win over a large number of first-time banking customers (and convinces its older user base that banking can indeed provide a pleasant user experience), its market share should continue to grow. It's worth noting that, according to our research, approximately 25% of Revolut users under the age of 35 use Revolut as their primary account. We will discuss this in more detail later, but regardless of other factors, the maturation of this group alone should have a profound impact on the future of market share in the European banking sector. ARPU also has significant growth potential. Another growth driver—ARPU (Average Revenue Per User)—has even greater room for growth. Changes in customer wallet share in the financial services sector typically take decades, not years. Revolut continues to earn customer trust, with its primary account users (according to company reports) growing by 45%, exceeding the overall user growth rate of 30%. Rapid growth in primary account users is crucial because, in terms of average revenue per user (ARPU), primary account users represent the greatest benefit: Our research shows that existing banking institutions (with established customer relationships) can increase their primary account share to over 60%. Similarly, Revolut primary account users report that they spend and save approximately twice as much on their primary accounts as on any other account they have—and spending tends to increase as customers age; In short, more (and more established) primary users translate to higher ARPU, and Revolut's growing primary share has a fairly high ceiling, comparable to existing banks. Another aspect of Revolut's growing "primary" relationship is its untapped loan revenue opportunities: As mentioned above, Revolut currently derives 76% of its revenue from fees, compared to approximately 30% for established banks; and as of the end of 2025, Revolut's loan-to-deposit ratio (LDR) was only around 6%, while traditional banks typically have LDRs of 70% to 90% or more (approximately 4% based on total customer balance). Revolut's loan balance is projected to roughly triple by 2025 and will continue to grow for many years to come. Admittedly, robust loan growth takes time. However, based on current ceilings, Revolut has ample opportunity to further increase its average revenue per user (ARPU) by leveraging its balance sheet and offering customers higher-quality loan products. In comparison, a simple estimate of Barclays' UK consumer and corporate banking business suggests that its ARPU is around £435, approximately six times that of Revolut's current ARPU. To illustrate this, let's look at where Revolut currently stands in terms of coverage (i.e., penetration) and depth (i.e., share with major banks): [Image of Revolut's profile: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7442051_watermarknone.png](https://img.jinse.com.cn/7442051_watermarknone.png) [Image of Revolut's profile: RjTw1o33WyHJZtMBX22cfNxwJ6yuSZRTosukEx6u ...) [Image of Revolut's profile: RjTw1o33WyHJZtMBX22cfNxwJ6yuSZRTosukEx6u.png) [Image of Revolut's profile: RjTw1o33WyHJZtMBX22cfNxwJ6yuSZRTosukEx6u.png](https://img.jinse.com.cn/7442051_watermarknone.png) [Image of Revolut's profile: RjTw1o3 As the younger user base matures, the latter should happen naturally.

V. Conclusion: No Longer Just a Challenger

Revolut's 2025 data is important not only because it is impressive, but also because it paints a complete picture of a financial institution, not just a "challenger" bank.

Customer growth remains strong, the profit model continues to expand, master account adoption continues to rise, and profitability continues to improve, while the company continues to invest and expand rapidly. This combination is extremely rare in the financial services industry (or indeed in any industry).

There is still much work to be done—particularly in lending, regulation, and expanding into new markets—but after reading this annual report, we believe the question is less about “Can Revolut become a scalable banking platform” and more about “How large can this platform grow?” The company’s announced long-term goal is “100 million daily active users in 100 countries.” Currently, this goal is progressing steadily.

data.title: Circle plunges 20%: A draft sparks a crypto earthquake.
data.description: On March 24, 2026, Circle CEO Jeremy Allaire likely experienced one of the most difficult trading days since the company's IPO. The stablecoin company he co-founded saw its stock price plummet by one-fifth during the day. Over 30 million shares changed hands that day, the panic clearly written on the charts. And what caused all of this was a few pages of paper leaked from an office building in Washington, D.C. To understand this crash, we must first understand how Circle actually makes money. Many people think stablecoin companies are tech companies, but Circle does more like a bank. You give it $1, it gives you 1 USDC token. You can freely transfer and spend the token on the blockchain, while it uses your dollars to buy US Treasury bonds. The interest on these bonds is Circle's profit. How profitable is this business? In the fourth quarter of 2025, reserve interest alone contributed $733 million. The circulating supply of USDC surged 72% in a year, reaching $78 billion. Circle holds a $78 billion pool of funds, with interest flowing in like a tap, non-stop. Simply attracting reserves isn't enough; they need to get more people willing to exchange their money for USDC. So Circle and Coinbase made a deal: Circle would share a portion of its interest income with Coinbase, and Coinbase would then return a 3.5% annualized return to users holding USDC. You put your money in, and it earns interest effortlessly. You don't need to understand blockchain, you don't need to do anything, and the returns are automatically credited to your account. This model forms a beautiful flywheel: returns attract users, users bring in funds, funds generate interest, and interest feeds back into the returns. The flywheel spun faster and faster, and Circle's stock price surged from around $50 in early February to $135, a 170% increase in six weeks. Market expectations that the Federal Reserve would maintain high interest rates also helped Circle; the higher the interest rate, the more lucrative the reserve interest. Then, someone set their sights on this machine. The banking industry's counterattack. On March 24, the latest draft of the US Senate's CLARITY Act was revealed. This legislation, officially titled the "Digital Asset Markets Clarity Act," was originally intended to regulate the crypto market and had already passed a version in the House of Representatives. However, the Senate added an amendment to the latest version: prohibiting any platform from providing returns, directly or indirectly, for passively held stablecoins, including any arrangement that is economically or functionally equivalent to bank deposit interest. In short, your "earn interest on holding cryptocurrencies" scheme is no longer allowed. This ban hits Circle where it hurts. If returns can't be paid, no one will have the incentive to hold USDC in the short term, and in the long term, Coinbase's stablecoin business will shrink. Coinbase earned $364 million in stablecoin revenue in the fourth quarter, a sum now hanging in the balance. The power struggle behind the ban is more interesting than the ban itself. This battle over stablecoin yields has actually been going on for almost a year. The GENIUS Act, signed into effect in July 2025, prohibits issuers from paying interest directly to holders, but the law does not explicitly cover related parties and third-party platforms. Circle and Coinbase exploited this gray area: Circle shared its reserve interest with Coinbase, which then distributed it to users as "rewards," ensuring the money still reached their accounts after going through a loop. More than 40 banking associations, led by the American Bankers Association, subsequently sent a joint letter to Congress demanding that this loophole be closed. The yield ban in the latest draft of the Clarity Act is a direct product of this lobbying effort. The traditional banking industry in the United States is driving this amendment. The logic is simple: if Coinbase can offer a 3.5% yield, why would customers keep their money in traditional bank current accounts? This yield mechanism of stablecoins directly undermines the foundation of bank deposits. Reportedly, bank representatives were scheduled to review the draft text on the day it was released. The crypto industry wants to do what banks do; banks will never sit idly by and watch their deposits be siphoned away by a group of coders. Lawmakers on Capitol Hill need to choose between two forces, and at least judging from this draft bill, traditional forces have temporarily gained the upper hand. Shay Boloor, chief market strategist at Futurum Equities, points out a more crucial issue: this ban blocks USDC's path from a payment instrument to a "store of value." And that upgrade path was precisely the core logic behind Circle's soaring stock price in recent weeks. The 170% increase was based on the market's pricing in the future potential of USDC. Now that potential has been slashed by the draft bill, the 170% increase has become the best reason to short it. The stock price plummeted from a high of $125 to around $101, marking its biggest single-day drop since its June 2025 IPO. Coinbase, also dragged down, fell by about 10%. Tether's "taking advantage of your weakness" To make matters worse, on the same day, Circle's biggest competitor, Tether, announced it had hired a Big Four accounting firm to conduct a formal audit of all its USDT reserves. This news might normally be just gossip, but at this time, its impact is amplified tenfold. The stablecoin landscape has always been clearly defined: Tether's USDT has the largest scale, but its reserve transparency is heavily questioned; Circle's USDC is smaller, but its compliance is solid. Institutional investors choose USDC largely because they are buying "peace of mind." Circle's moat ultimately boils down to trust. Now Tether is addressing this weakness. Once the audit is passed, the biggest gap between USDT and USDC will be closed. Regulators are digging at Circle's moat, while competitors are building their own. This double whammy, coupled with the precise timing, is hard to call a coincidence. On the eve of a reshaping of the stablecoin regulatory landscape, Tether's decision to reveal its sincerity at this window is a shrewd move. Some market analysts bluntly state that if Tether obtains the backing of the Big Four audits and further expands its presence in the US market, USDC's market share among institutional investors will be further eroded. Circle's carefully cultivated image as a "compliance champion" over the past few years is turning from a unique advantage into an entry barrier. Armor and Shackles. However, there are also sobering voices. Clear Street analyst Owen Lau believes the market reaction is excessive. He says the actual situation isn't as bad as the headlines suggest, and is more like a knee-jerk reaction to complex legislative news. He has a point; the CLARITY Act is currently just a draft in the Senate, and a long process of negotiation is needed before it becomes law. While the Trump administration is pushing for the bill's passage, the revenue restriction clause itself could become an obstacle. The revenue restriction may be revised or even removed entirely in the final text. The draft doesn't offer a blanket ban: rewards linked to payments, transfers, and promotional activities can still be offered, but passive income is no longer allowed. The SEC, CFTC, and Treasury will further define what constitutes "permitted rewards" within a year of the bill's enactment; the details are still pending. If Circle can transform its business model from "holding for rewards" to "using for rewards," the game might continue. Furthermore, USDC's growth isn't entirely driven by revenue. Polymarket, the world's largest prediction market, runs on USDC, and this transactional demand won't simply disappear because of a ban. Circle also launched Arc last year, a Layer-1 blockchain designed for stablecoin financial scenarios, covering global payments, foreign exchange, and asset tokenization, attempting to expand its business from stablecoin issuance to financial infrastructure. USDC won't die, but whether it can maintain the growth rate of the past year is a big question mark. Looking back, this crash served as a wake-up call for all crypto companies. In the past few years, the most successful companies in the crypto industry have all embraced regulation. Circle was a model student of this approach: proactive IPO, transparent audits, and active lobbying—all proving to Wall Street that it was a financial innovator in a suit. The market rewarded it; in less than a year since its IPO, its stock price has multiplied several times. Fourth-quarter revenue reached $770 million, a 76.9% year-over-year increase, with earnings per share of $0.43, significantly exceeding market expectations of $0.25. On paper, this is a rapidly growing, high-performing company. However, the CLARITY Act draft exposes an uncomfortable reality: compliance means voluntarily placing yourself within the regulatory range. Tether, operating overseas, is not directly affected by the ban, but Circle, as a US-listed company, can only obediently accept scrutiny. Compliance has given Circle armor, but also shackles. The bill is still under negotiation; the story is far from over. But this day in March 2026 is worth remembering: when innovation in the crypto world touches the boundaries of traditional finance, which way the scales tip in Capitol Hill ultimately depends on who has the bigger voice in Washington.

data.title: Web3 is sick, but the cure isn't AI.
data.description:
Author:Zhou, ChainCatcher

Over the past year,AI technology breakthroughs have far exceeded expectations.GPT-4oTo the emergence of variousAI Agentstools, new capabilities are being broken every few months.. The explosive popularity of next-generation AI Agent products, such as OpenClaw, has made many crypto professionals realize for the first time that AI is beginning to challenge the boundaries of their work. Thus, an anxiety surrounding "AI" has spread throughout the crypto community. From macro-strategies to micro-work methods, from leading institutions to frontline practitioners, many crypto projects and practitioners have begun to re-examine their own value and work models—will their work be replaced? Does the industry have a future? These questions have been repeatedly raised over the past year. I. Project Teams and Institutions Panic First [H2 style="text-align: left;">Product Responses

WhenAI Agentcan directly take over the keyboard and mouse, and perform tasks on the computer, users will be able to complete on-chain operations directly throughAI without needing to open exchanges or wallets. So, where will the value of exchanges' entry points be reflected?

Earlier this month, leading exchanges such as OKX, Binance, and Coinbase announced their AI product plans in quick succession.

Among them,OKX launched Onchain OS AI Upgrade, support AI Agent Across 60 Multiple blockchains and 500 

Decentralization AI Computing network Gonka Co-founder David and Daniil Liberman at

data.title: Why did Circle, a stablecoin not allowed to "pay interest," plummet by 20%?
data.description:
US stablecoin regulation is shifting from "boundary delineation" to "structural constraints."

In the newly disclosed draft amendments to the Clarity Act, a seemingly technical clause has triggered a rapid pricing adjustment in the capital markets:Prohibits users from earning returns solely for holding stablecoins and restricts any arrangements that are economically equivalent to deposit interest. As a result, Circle (CRCL), the issuer of the USD stablecoin USDC, fell by about 20% at one point during the day, and Coinbase (COIN) also came under pressure. If this is simply interpreted as "tightening regulation," it's easy to underestimate the actual meaning of this clause. More accurately, this is a "targeted constraint" on the core mechanism of the stablecoin business model.

“Yield Penetration” Model Restricted

Stablecoins have long been in a relatively ambiguous regulatory space: on the one hand, they are not recognized as bank deposits; on the other hand, their funding structure is highly similar to “deposit-like liabilities”.

Circle Under Pressure

For companies like Circle that heavily rely on interest income from reserve assets, this constraint directly targets the core of their revenue structure.

“Scale-driven logic” weakened

The growth of stablecoins is usually driven by two types of demand:

  • PayFi Strategic Blueprint

    It is worth noting that Circle's business layout has already demonstrated a forward-looking response to the aforementioned changes to some extent.Circle is committed to building a "Web3 PayFi network", and its strategic goal is to compete with traditional payment giant Stripe, attempting to reduce its reliance on the revenue from a single reserve through diversified services ...>

    PayFi Strategic Blueprint

    It is worth noting that Circle's business layout has already demonstrated a forward-looking response to the aforementioned changes to some extent.Circle is committed to building a "Web3 PayFi network", and its strategic goal is to compete with traditional payment giant Stripe, attempting to reduce its reliance on the revenue from a single reserve through diversified services.

    Its current strategy can be summarized in three levels: 1. Infrastructure: Arc. As the underlying network for on-chain asset issuance and circulation, Arc has processed over 1.6... 1. Billions of transactions, handling cross-chain and liquidity distribution functions. 2. Payment Network: CPN (Circle Payments Network) Annualized transaction volume of approximately $5.7 billion, with over 50 financial institutions connected, attempting to build a settlement system similar to traditional payment networks.

    3. Application Layer (including AI Payment and Micro-payment)

    Through products such as Nanopayments, support high-frequency transactions of extremely low amounts (as low as one millionth of a dollar), providing infrastructure for automated payment scenarios (such as AI Agents). In addition, Circle leverages its strong corporate partnership network to collaborate with industry giants such as Visa, Intuit, Polymarket, JPMorgan Chase, and Mastercard to jointly launch products utilizing USDC and continuously expand its application scenarios. These strategic initiatives demonstrate that Circle is not content with being merely a "stablecoin issuer," but is committed to becoming the "Stripe" of the Web3 world, generating revenue from diversified sources such as transactions themselves, payment services, and developer tools by building underlying payment infrastructure. From a policy perspective, the direction of the Clarity Act is not complicated: to remove stablecoins from the category of "deposit-like instruments" to prevent them from substituting for the funding sources of the banking system, while retaining their function as payment and settlement tools. In other words, regulation is not negating stablecoins, but rather reshaping their functional boundaries. Under this framework, the industry may diverge: The model relying on interest rate spreads and capital accumulation will face continuous compression. Models relying on transactions, clearing, and network effects possess stronger sustainability. If the restrictions of the Clarity Act are ultimately implemented, their impact will extend beyond short-term market fluctuations; it will be a systemic reshaping of the stablecoin business model. For Circle, this represents both a direct compression of its existing interest rate spread model and a passive path shift. Once "profitability" is constrained, what truly determines its long-term value will no longer be the interest rate, but rather: its ability to become the infrastructure for global capital flows.

data.title: Those who couldn't buy Anthropic drove up the price of its related stocks to 16 times.
data.description:
Author: David｜Deep Tide TechFlow

Last Thursday, a new stock was added to the NYSE, ticker symbol VCX.

It is actually a fund. The fund holds shares in companies such as Anthropic, OpenAI, and SpaceX. Anthropic accounts for 21%, and OpenAI accounts for 10%.

These companies have one thing in common: they are not publicly traded, and ordinary people cannot buy their shares.

VCX is currently one of the very few things on the market that allows ordinary investors to indirectly hold Anthropic shares.

VCX is currently one of the very few things on the market that allows ordinary investors to indirectly hold Anthropic shares.

Its net asset value is $19 per share. On its first day of trading, it opened at $42, surged to $125 during the day, and closed at $76. On the fourth trading day, it reached a high of $315 during the day, triggering circuit breakers twice. In four days, it rose from $19 to $315.

Investors are essentially buying this fund at 16 times its actual asset value. Not because the fund manager is particularly skilled, but because Anthropic is involved.

A month ago, Anthropic raised $30 billion at a valuation of $380 billion, the second-largest fundraising globally this year. Its annualized revenue is $14 billion. But it's not publicly traded, has no stock ticker, and you won't find it in any brokerage's search box.

If you can't buy the real thing, you'll try to grab its shadow. VCX is currently just a shadow of Anthropic, or rather, a shadow of AI FOMO (Fear of Missing Out). Why is it so expensive? VCX isn't a fund in the traditional sense. With ordinary funds, if you think it's expensive, you can wait for it to drop because fund managers can issue more shares; the supply is elastic. VCX is a closed-end fund; the number of shares is locked up when it's listed and won't increase. More importantly, the vast majority of shares cannot be sold at all. Investors who bought before February 20th have their shares locked for six months, meaning they won't be able to trade until September. VCX has over 100,000 investors, but only a small fraction is currently available for trading. What does this mean? Many people want to buy, but very few can. Even a small number of buy orders can distort the price. Therefore, the 16x premium actually reflects the sheer number of people wanting to get involved with Anthropic, and how narrow the entry barrier is. However, this hunger wasn't created by VCX itself.

Chart: Fundrise's VCX Fund Holdings TOP10

Over the past decade, a structural change has occurred in the technology industry:The best companies are going public later and later, or even not going public at all.

Over the past decade, the technology industry has undergone a structural change:The best companies are going public later and later, or even not going public at all.

When Facebook went public in 2012, its valuation was $104 billion, an astronomical figure at the time. Today, Anthropic's private equity valuation is more than three times that of Facebook's IPO, yet it didn't even have a clear plan to go public before. OpenAI is valued at $500 billion but hasn't gone public either. Rumors of SpaceX preparing for an IPO have been circulating for over a year, but there's still no definite date. Ten years ago, a company that reached this size would have already rung the bell on the NYSE. Now they don't need to. The private market provides almost unlimited funding, without the pressure of quarterly reports or dealing with retail investors and short sellers. For founders, this is a rational choice. For ordinary investors, this means that you can only watch some of the fastest-growing companies in history from behind a glass wall. VCX was originally scheduled to list on March 9th, but it was postponed by ten days due to the Iran war. In those ten days, nothing changed—Anthropic neither increased nor decreased in price, and the fund's holdings remained unchanged. But the postponement itself created another ten days of anticipation. On the day it finally listed, all the pent-up demand from those ten days squeezed into an extremely narrow channel. Not all shadows are valuable. If you want to access stocks of companies that aren't publicly listed, VCX funds aren't the only option. Before discussing these avenues, there's a more fundamental question: Anthropic isn't publicly traded, so how did a publicly traded fund acquire its shares? The answer is the back door. Large private companies typically conduct a funding round every few months, from Series A to Series G, bringing in new investors in each round. Anthropic just closed a $30 billion Series G round last month, with a long list of participating institutions ranging from GIC to Sequoia to Goldman Sachs. These rounds are usually only open to institutional investors, with entry thresholds often starting at tens of millions of dollars. But there's a second way. The fact that a company isn't publicly listed doesn't mean its shares can't be traded privately. Early employees and angel investors hold shares, and some want to cash out early. This creates a secondary market for private companies—unpublic and opaque, but transactions still occur. Fundrise started buying through these two avenues in 2022, when private tech company valuations had just plummeted, making them cheap. Over four years, they built a portfolio including Anthropic, OpenAI, and SpaceX. Then they put it into a VCX, listed it on the NYSE, and ordinary people could buy it like stocks. In the same month, at least three similar funds traded on the NYSE, all selling the same concept: selling you what you bought through the back door through the front door. Robinhood launched a fund called RVI, which went public on March 6th with an offering price of $25. Its holdings included Databricks, Revolut, and Ramp, all reputable private companies. It fell 11% on its first day of trading, closing at $21. Destiny Tech100, ticker symbol DXYZ, went public in 2024, making it a pioneer in this sector. It has a significant stake in SpaceX, accounting for 16% of its portfolio. I only indirectly increased my exposure to Anthropic this February. The stock price is currently hovering around $24. There's also XOVR, the first ETF approved to directly hold equity in a private company, with SpaceX accounting for approximately 21%. These four funds have similar structures and concepts, and are all traded on the same exchange. But their fates are completely different.

VCX rose 1500% in four days. RVI broke its IPO price on its first day. DXYZ remained lukewarm.

VCX holds 21% of Anthropic and 10% of OpenAI. RVI's holdings contain neither Anthropic nor OpenAI. DXYZ's Anthropic exposure was recently added and is a very small percentage.

VCX holds 21% of Anthropic and 10% of OpenAI. RVI's holdings contain neither Anthropic nor OpenAI. DXYZ's Anthropic exposure is recent and is very small.

This shows that, at least for now, the market isn't actually scrambling for "shares in private companies." The market is scrambling for Anthropic. Whoever is closer to them is valuable. Robinhood's RVI lost because of this. Databricks and Revolut are certainly good companies, but clearly, right now, they aren't the names that make people willing to pay a 16x premium. Even shadows have an expiration date. What were the people who bought VCX at $312 betting on? The gamble is that before the door opens, someone will still be willing to pay a higher price to avoid getting Anthropic. However, this door won't stay closed forever. VCX has over 100,000 investors, the vast majority of whom have their shares locked up for six months. The lock-up period ends on September 19th. At that time, a large number of shares will flood the market, and the supply will go from extremely scarce to abundant overnight. VCX was able to sell at a 16x premium, partly because it contained Anthropic, and perhaps partly because the available shares were too small. Once the lock-up period ends, the second condition disappears. There's an even bigger variable. Anthropic, OpenAI, and SpaceX are all rumored to be IPOs between the second half of 2026 and 2027. Anthropic just raised $30 billion last month, valuing the company at $380 billion, and has already hired Silicon Valley law firm Wilson Sonsini to prepare for its listing. SpaceX's CFO has been communicating with investors about its IPO since the end of last year, with a target of mid-year. Once the main players go public, the shadows become worthless. If you can directly search for Anthropic's stock code in a brokerage search box, why would you pay a 16x premium to buy a fund that indirectly holds it? For example, as mentioned earlier, when DXYZ first went public in 2024, it also experienced a surge in price. Later, SpaceX's delayed IPO caused the hype to subside, and its stock price fell by more than half from its peak. Therefore, VCX investors are experiencing a classic countdown. What they are buying at 16x the price isn't Anthropic shares, but a ticket with an expiration date. When the door opens depends on when Anthropic decides to go public. Before that, the premium was maintained by scarcity; after that, the premium went to zero. But the phenomenon of "shadow stocks" is not accidental. Every wave of technological advancement creates the same anxiety: you can't buy the most important companies. In the 2000s, before Google's IPO, Goldman Sachs employees were scrambling for allocations internally. In 2020, it was SpaceX, and Silicon Valley secondary market intermediaries suddenly became the most sought-after connections. Now it's AI's turn. This time, the anxiety is deeper. Anthropic and OpenAI may not be making money now, but they are rewriting the rules. Because of the impact of AI, SaaS stocks have crashed, security stocks have crashed, and IBM lost $31 billion in a single day. Investors see more than just "this company is making money"; they see "if I'm not on its side, I might be on the side it crushes." The 16x premium of VCX isn't just pricing in a fund; it's pricing in this anxiety itself. Tickets will expire, and premiums will fade. But as long as AI continues to accelerate, and as long as the most valuable companies remain closed, there will be people willing to pay irrational prices for their shadows. It's not because the shadow is worth the money, it's because the feeling of being locked out is too expensive.

data.title: A 43-year-old billionaire has passed away; OnlyFans, which he effectively controlled, had explored the world of crypto.
data.description:
Author: Michael Mach Source: X, @1912212eth

OnlyFans once purchased tens of millions of dollars worth of ETH.

On March 23, Leonid Radvinsky, the actual controller of OnlyFans, passed away at the age of 43 after a long battle with cancer. A spokesperson for OnlyFans only said "passed away peacefully" and requested that the public respect the privacy of his family.

OnlyFans is a content subscription platform headquartered in London, UK. Creators upload exclusive content, and fans pay monthly subscriptions. It focuses on adult content creators but also covers fitness, music, cooking, and other fields. According to the latest data in 2025, OnlyFans user accounts have stabilized at approximately 377.5 million, and the number of creators has grown to 4.63 million.

Leonid Radvinsky

Little is known about Radvinsky's stance on crypto—he hasn't publicly endorsed any blockchain, but he has explored the crypto world.

Leonid Radvinsky

The outside world knows very little about Radvinsky's attitude towards crypto—he hasn't publicly endorsed any blockchain, but he has explored the crypto world.

Having purchased tens of millions of dollars worth of ETH and donated to the Ukrainian DAO, OnlyFans quietly launched a small feature in February 2022 during his tenure: supporting Ethereum-verified NFTs as avatars. The platform explicitly stated that this was "the first step in exploring the role of NFTs on the platform." Creators' NFT avatars would have a small Ethereum icon, which could be clicked to jump to OpenSea for details. This seemingly insignificant feature allowed millions of adult creators to directly access crypto assets for the first time. This move was early. That year, the NFT craze was at its peak; Twitter launched NFT avatars, and OnlyFans quickly followed suit. What is little known is that OnlyFans' parent company, Fenix ​​International, also purchased tens of millions of dollars worth of ETH. According to the company's financial statements as of the end of November 2022, Fenix ​​purchased a total of approximately $19.9 million worth of ETH, classifying it as intangible assets. Due to the cryptocurrency market crash in 2022, the price of Ethereum plummeted, and the company recorded an impairment loss of approximately $8.46 million that year, adjusting the value of its ETH holdings to approximately $11.4 million. The OnlyFans platform still does not support crypto payments; users rely on credit cards or third-party virtual cards. During Radvinsky's era, OnlyFans was more like a hybrid of "traditional finance + adult content," taking a 20% cut and maintaining an incredibly stable cash flow. Turning our attention to the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022, a group of cryptocurrency activists and enthusiasts quickly rallied to support Ukraine. Their method was to auction off an NFT artwork depicting the Ukrainian flag. An auction held by the decentralized autonomous organization "Ukraine DAO" raised 2,258 Ether, worth approximately $6.79 million at the time. According to a subsequent report by Decrypt, the adult website OnlyFans also participated in the donation. Blockchain tracking platform Etherscan shows that on February 27th, an address named only.eth donated 500 ETH to the Ukrainian DAO, now worth $1.079 million.

Etherscan transfer screenshot

Zapper data shows that the only.eth address was created in May 2021, and its current wallet value is less than $2,000. The last wallet transfer activity was 3 years ago.

However, it was its founder, Tim Stokely, who truly brought the OnlyFans DNA into cryptography.

Tim Stokely

In 2016, Stokely founded the platform OnlyFans. In December 2021, he resigned as CEO, making a complete career change. In May 2022, he and former OnlyFans executive RJ Phillips launched Zoop—an NFT trading card platform based on the Polygon blockchain.

... Zoop is positioned as completely "family-friendly": selling 3D digital collectible cards featuring celebrities and influencers, which users can buy, sell, trade, and collect, and even receive airdrop rewards. It features limited releases and emphasizes revenue sharing for creators. Essentially, Zoop brings OnlyFans' "direct payment to fans" model to the Web3 collectibles market. In April 2025, Zoop, in conjunction with the HBAR Foundation (the treasury manager of the Hedera blockchain), submitted a bid to acquire TikTok's US operations. The core of the proposal is Web3 integration: Hedera's scalability will be used to support TikTok's NFTs, creator payments, governance structure, and overall token incentive system, with the goal of directly distributing 80% of advertising revenue to creators and users. Ultimately, for various reasons, the intended bid did not go through. Tim Stokely, having withdrawn from the adult entertainment industry, brought his core logic to the TikTok bidding for Zoop and Hedera, essentially upgrading the "fans pay for content" model to a Web3 version.

data.title: How to build the strongest Ethereum? Reshaping the relationship between L1 and L2
data.description:
Authors: Josh Rudolf, Julian Ma, Josh Stark Source: Ethereum Foundation Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Key Takeaways

Goal: All users (individuals and institutions) should have a clear path to leverage, extend, and benefit from Ethereum's core features.

The optimal path to achieving this goal is to fully leverage the unique capabilities of each layer, solidify Ethereum's core attributes, and create tangible value for end users through these attributes.

As the ecosystem develops, the roles of each layer are constantly evolving:

  • In the past: L2's primary goal was to scale Ethereum, followed by leaving room for differentiation and customization; the core focus was on scaling.

    • In the past: L2's primary goal was to scale Ethereum, followed by leaving room for differentiation and customization; the core focus was on scaling.

      • Today: The primary goal of L2 is to provide differentiated features, services, customization options, market strategies, and controllable domains, while also offering additional scaling capabilities. The core focus has shifted to differentiation, control, and innovation. L1's positioning: As a truly permissionless and highly resilient global hub, it undertakes the core functions of settlement, shared state, liquidity, and DeFi. A robust L1 that is uncompromising on CROPS (censorship resistance, open source, privacy, and security) and continuously scaling provides a more solid foundation for L2. L2's positioning: To provide valuable new features, customization options, and control, developing its own on-chain economy while extending Ethereum's core features to more users. A strong L2 network will feed back into the ecosystem, consolidating Ethereum's central position. L2 platforms come in various forms, and their degree of integration with L1 can be flexibly adjusted according to their own needs. Integration with L1: L2 platforms aiming for deep integration with L1 should move towards mechanisms such as synchronous composability, full interoperability, shared liquidity, Stage 2, and native Rollups. Coverage: Various L2 platforms with diverse business models and technical expertise will continue to play an important role in the ecosystem, providing capabilities that L1 lacks. The Ethereum Foundation (EF) will continue to invest in technological research and development to enable L2 platforms to more seamlessly extend the core attributes of L1 and securely access liquidity and funds across L1 and other L2 platforms. L2 platforms should maintain the transparency and verifiability of their security attributes. In short, both sides bear important missions, and their ideals and practices should be consistent.

        Introduction

        Over the past five years, a number of blockchain ecosystems have grown around Ethereum L1. These chains can choose to inherit different characteristics of Ethereum: some fully inherit decentralization (such as Stage 2 Rollups), some inherit some security attributes (such as Validium, Prividium), and others are built solely on the general EVM standard (not L2). Many projects are still under development, often starting as independent chains and gradually integrating deeply with Ethereum L1.

        Now, the Ethereum Foundation and the broader Ethereum ecosystem need to update our understanding of the relationship between L1 and L2 networks. The last systematic review was about five years ago, when a roadmap centered on Rollups was first proposed as the direction for Ethereum scaling.

        Since then, the industry has undergone tremendous changes: the technology supporting L2 sharing Ethereum security and liquidity and achieving interoperability has matured and evolved; the competitive advantages and user value of L2 have become increasingly clear; L2 itself has grown and developed into an independent ecosystem and community; and the L1 scaling roadmap has become clearer and more defined. As an ecosystem community, we need to face these changes squarely and learn from the successes and failures of the ecosystem. In recent months, the future relationship between Ethereum L1 and L2 has become increasingly clear: A thriving Ethereum ecosystem must be rooted in a strong L1 foundation. Ethereum L1 will achieve orders-of-magnitude scaling while retaining the highest level of security and decentralization, continuing to serve as the heart of the on-chain economy and a DeFi hub. The independent and interoperable L2 ecosystem will continue to expand, providing a high degree of customization, control, and functionality that L1 cannot achieve. These L2s choose to be rooted in the Ethereum ecosystem because it is the optimal solution for their users, community, and businesses. L2s both compete and cooperate (which is healthy and positive), providing diverse and specialized block spaces, services, and assets. This article aims to elaborate on the development vision of L1 and L2 and build a mutually empowering cooperative path for Ethereum L1 and all chains that wish to integrate into the ecosystem. What are the positioning of L1 and L2, and how do they collaborate? Ethereum L1 is the world's leading programmable blockchain. In terms of adoption rate, developer attention, decentralization, resilience, and robustness, no other chain can currently rival it. Ethereum L1 is the core of the DeFi ecosystem, possessing the deepest liquidity. Currently, Ethereum L1 has a clear scaling path while adhering to decentralization and robustness. Thanks to the efforts of numerous teams within the ecosystem, the development speed of zero-knowledge (ZK) technology has exceeded expectations. In the coming years, Ethereum L1 can achieve scaling by several orders of magnitude without compromising its core values ​​and vision. However, at the same time, no single chain can meet the diverse needs of a globalized on-chain economy. Even if Ethereum remains the world's leading blockchain and achieves a thousandfold increase in scaling, the market will still need a large number of different chains—because they can provide specialization and customization capabilities that no L1 blockchain can achieve: Specialized design for specific applications and scenarios; Non-EVM functionality; Stronger privacy protection; Pricing mechanisms or transaction packaging logic; Ultra-low latency and other sorting features; Extreme scaling capabilities unattainable by L1 blockchains; Specialized economic models, market strategies, and growth methods. Modular design to support compliance or other business needs. Other improvements and innovations that can be iterated and implemented faster beyond L1. Governance strategies that give stakeholders fine-grained control over their flexible execution environment on Ethereum. This creates an opportunity for Ethereum L1 and L2 to establish a mutually beneficial relationship, with each fulfilling its role and complementing each other's strengths. Why should other chains become L2 on Ethereum? High security, low adversary risk, high decentralization, and significantly reduced costs. L2 can achieve the highest security and decentralization at a cost far lower than replacing L1. Building and incentivizing a global decentralized validator integration is costly, time-consuming, and extremely difficult. L2 can transfer this responsibility to Ethereum L1, paying based on usage, rather than bearing the huge fixed costs of building its own L1 validator network. Users and developers can reach more users and developers by interoperating with the largest L1 and largest L2 networks (ZK technology, real-time proofs, faster L1 finality and L2 settlement, and mature smart proxy infrastructure will further accelerate interoperability and cross-chain experiences). A well-designed interoperability L2 can securely access L1 assets, DeFi liquidity, L1 user accounts, and L1 services such as oracles and ENS. Marketing benefits include the brand and reputation advantages of being part of the Ethereum ecosystem—Ethereum boasts the strongest reputation, security record, and regulatory approval among all L1 platforms. How can Ethereum L1 benefit from this relationship? Based on experience and communication with various stakeholders in the ecosystem, we believe that placing Ethereum L1 at the center of the ever-expanding L2 network will solidify Ethereum and ETH's unique position in the on-chain economy: Creating demand for ETH and enabling trust-free, secure cross-chain bridging between ETH and other assets. ETH simultaneously functions as a store of value, currency, and application platform within the Ethereum ecosystem. Amplifying Ethereum's network effects (such as EVM, developer education, toolchains, user onboarding, and L2 interoperability). Consolidating Ethereum's value as the core of the multi-chain ecosystem and the primary settlement and liquidity layer for the on-chain economy. L2 will help Ethereum achieve its core vision: to serve as a distribution engine for Ethereum's core attributes (security, resilience, robustness) (providing additional scaling), allowing more people to sustainably benefit from Ethereum. The Ethereum ecosystem should not take these benefits for granted. Some viewpoints remain controversial within the community, and some long-standing arguments still need to be verified through experimentation, measurement, and analysis. Ultimately, the relationship between L1 and L2 must be mutually beneficial to succeed. The first five years of this relationship have yielded many achievements and laid a key foundation for the future. What does this mean for the future development of L2? What does this new vision mean for L2, its team, and the community? Our recommendations are as follows: L2 platforms should focus on strategies that complement L1 platforms and differentiate themselves. Many L2 platforms have successfully implemented this approach: building unique communities and extending Ethereum features to millions of new users through innovative functionalities, vertical scenarios (such as application chains), new distribution models, or novel marketing strategies. L2 platforms should boldly explore all feasible directions for differentiation. Emerging directions include scalability, trustlessness, privacy, corporate compliance, industry verticals, community operations, and various technological innovations. Other suitable scenarios for L2 platforms include encrypted electronic voting bulletin boards and certificate transparency systems. L2 platforms can choose to fully or partially inherit Ethereum attributes based on their own goals, which is a reasonable choice, but users must clearly understand the security attributes they provide and do not provide.

      • A trustless L2 should at least reach Stage 1 and pass the withdrawal test: even if there are malicious operators or the security committee fails, users can still safely withdraw to L1. Choose the L2 that is closest to L1 and fully inherits its attributes. The following directions should be taken: Implement Stage 2. Achieve synchronous composability between the protocol layer and the application layer (including read-only operations like L1SLOAD/L1STATICCALL and read-write operations). Become a native Rollup (removing the security committee). L2 should continuously promote interoperability and shared liquidity mechanisms to strengthen the entire system. We encourage teams to research the Open Intents Framework and Fast Confirmation Rule, explore designs that allow L1 funds to be accessed without leaving L2, and participate in work related to synchronous composability. L2 should operate transparently, clearly explaining its security attributes and relevance to L1 to the ecosystem (L2Beat plays a crucial role in improving L2 ecosystem transparency and driving continuous improvement). The Ethereum Foundation's Work in This Area: To realize this vision of the relationship between L1 and L2, the Ethereum Foundation has clarified its mission and is advancing the following work: Promoting the scaling of L1 and Blob data without sacrificing decentralization and robustness. Currently, Blob utilization is only about 30%, leaving ample room for growth and the potential for significant scaling if necessary. **Focus on supporting L2 platforms that already possess or wish to enhance capabilities in core areas such as privacy, security, and trustlessness.** **Led by Josh Rudolf, the platform team is optimizing the Ethereum platform as the interface between L2 and the core protocol roadmap.** **Improve L1 liquidity and simplify the process for L2 platforms to access liquidity (faster finality, withdrawals, and deposits).** **Work closely with L2 teams to understand their needs and prioritize them in the protocol, clarifying the relationship between L1 and L2. We need to identify what works and what doesn't, collaborate, and always clearly define and reinforce the value of being part of the Ethereum ecosystem.** **Invest in R&D to support native Rollup technology—L2 platforms that can be fully trustlessly verified by L1, enabling simultaneous composability and secure interoperability.** Working closely with organizations like L2Beat, we will monitor and validate L2 security attributes. We must rigorously and honestly disclose L2 attributes and the extent to which they share L1 security, enabling users and developers to make informed choices. Addressing a major drawback of multi-chain ecosystems: fragmentation. We will collaborate with ecosystem partners (chains, wallets, infrastructure providers) to create better interoperability solutions, improving user experience and alleviating fragmentation on developer platforms. As the vision for the relationship between L1 and L2 becomes clearer, we will also address the fragmentation of the Ethereum narrative.

data.title: Why did Tether suddenly undergo a full audit? Is the stablecoin era entering a transparent phase?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

On March 24, 2026, Tether announced that it had officially commissioned one of the Big Four accounting firms (Deloitte, EY, KPMG, and PwC) to conduct its first comprehensive independent financial statement audit. This audit is expected to be the largest first audit in the history of the financial market. Its scale is extremely rare outside of sovereign institutions, and it covers an extremely complex combination of digital assets, traditional reserves, and tokenized liabilities.

Tether CFO Simon McWilliams stated, "We selected this Big Four accounting firm through a competitive process; the audit will be completed smoothly." As of press time, Tether has not disclosed which accounting firm is conducting the audit.

Why is Tether conducting such a large-scale audit? What will be the impact?

I. Responding to the "Trust Crisis"

Previously, the market had a long-standing trust crisis regarding Tether—a lack of confidence in the authenticity of its reserves.

Last December, BitMEX founder Arthur Hayes warned that Tether could face serious problems if the value of USDT's reserve assets fell. In 2017, Tether pledged to conduct regular audits of its assets; however, in January 2018, the company terminated its partnership with its auditing firm (Friedman LLP) without completing the audit, exacerbating concerns about the source of USDT's funds. Around the same time, the closely related Bitfinex exchange experienced banking difficulties, unable to access $850 million in customer funds entrusted to payment processors. In 2019, the New York State Attorney General's Office (NYAG) launched an investigation into Tether and Bitfinex, accusing Bitfinex of jeopardizing USDT's funding by misappropriating Tether's dollar reserves to cover funding shortfalls. The dispute was settled in February 2021, with Tether's parent company, iFinex, agreeing to pay an $18.5 million fine to the New York State Attorney General's Office and cease trading with New York customers; however, neither company admitted to wrongdoing. Tether's transparency and reserve practices have been a focus of regulatory scrutiny. In October 2021, the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) fined Tether $41 million for making misleading statements about its reserves. The CFTC found that during certain periods between 2016 and 2018, Tether's fiat currency reserves were less than the circulating supply of USDT, contradicting its past public claims that each token had adequate reserves. Tether described the scope of this audit as "extremely comprehensive." The accounting firm responsible for the audit stated that the level of this audit "is typically only achieved by the world's largest national institutions." This audit will carefully examine Tether's complex asset portfolio, which primarily comprises three core components: Digital Assets: This category includes cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin and Ethereum held in its reserves. Traditional Reserves: These are traditional assets such as U.S. Treasury bills, commercial paper, and cash equivalents. Tokenized Government Bonds: This represents a newer, innovative asset class involving blockchain-based sovereign debt. Tether has a long history of trust crises, and this more comprehensive audit is expected to address these concerns. II. Regulatory Needs Stablecoins have evolved from crypto-transaction tools into a part of global payments and financial stability. The EU's MiCA Act, the US draft stablecoin bill, and various countries' regulatory requirements regarding anti-money laundering and the source of funds are constantly tightening. Transparent reserves, auditability, and near-bank regulation are the current consensus in the stablecoin industry. According to Cryptorank, Tether previously commissioned a smaller accounting firm, BDO Italia, to issue an assurance report, which only provided an overview of its assets. However, these assurance reports are fundamentally different from a full audit. Audits follow strict international standards and issue formal opinions on the fairness of financial statements. Assurance reports, on the other hand, only verify information at a specific point in time. Throughout 2024, regulators intensified their push for higher verification standards. Specifically, regulatory frameworks such as the EU's Crypto Asset Markets (MiCA) regulations and proposed US legislation began to mandate that stablecoin issuers comply with stringent reserve reporting and auditing requirements. Therefore, Tether's proactive measures appear strategically aligned with this global regulatory trend, demonstrating its preemptive approach to compliance requirements and establishing legitimacy. This move signifies a step towards greater financial transparency for Tether.

III. Responding to Competition from Circle

Tether remains the leading stablecoin in the cryptocurrency industry, with a market capitalization of approximately $184 billion, more than double that of Circle's USDC (which has a market capitalization of $78 billion). However, a report released earlier this month by Japanese investment bank Mizuho Bank stated that Circle's stablecoin trading volume has surpassed USDT's for the first time since 2019: USDC trading volume is approximately $2.2 trillion, while USDT trading volume is $1.3 trillion.

III. Responding to Competition from Circle

Tether remains the leading stablecoin in the cryptocurrency industry, with a market capitalization of approximately $184 billion, more than double that of Circle's USDC (which has a market capitalization of $78 billion).

Mizuho Bank stated, "Data shows that USDC accounts for 64% of trading volume relative to USDT. This reverses the long-term trend from 2019 to 2025 where USDT trading volume exceeded USDC." Back in June 2022, Circle CFO Jeremy Fox-Geen wrote an article titled "How to Build Trust," which explicitly mentioned that since its launch in 2018, the USDC reserve has been audited annually by a leading global accounting firm as part of Circle's annual financial statements. This audit aims to verify the accuracy, completeness, and composition of the reserve, and to test internal controls that ensure the accuracy of the financial statements. In addition to annual audits, Circle has published monthly reports on the size and composition of USDC reserves since its launch in 2018 to ensure that the reserve size is at least equivalent to the circulating supply of USDC. Circle's proactive cooperation with regulators, greater asset transparency, and closer alignment with the traditional banking system clearly make it easier to gain favor than Tether. The stablecoin market previously relied on liquidity and market share, but now transparency and compliance are paramount. If Tether does not conduct compliance audits, it will find itself in a passive position. Therefore, following Circle's lead in the stablecoin market, Tether has also had to embark on the path of compliance—to counter competition from compliant stablecoins like USDC and solidify its leading position through audit endorsements. IV. Institutional Entry and the Need for Transparency
Heavy participants in the crypto market have shifted from retail investors to institutions. Institutional funds are subject to strict constraints and rely more on audited financial information for decision-making; they must hold legal assets within a regulatory framework; if USDT is non-compliant, it cannot be held by institutions as an investable asset. This audit will allow USDT to be fully held by institutions. Furthermore, due to USDT's enormous size, as the largest source of liquidity in the crypto market, a foundational asset of DeFi, and a cross-border payment tool, even the slightest mistake by Tether could have a huge impact on the crypto and traditional financial markets. Market observers believe that any major misstep by the company could trigger a "black swan" collapse for the entire industry. During the deleveraging wave of 2022 and the FTX bankruptcy, there were particular concerns about such issues, but USDT ultimately maintained its pegged price and market position. Tether has become so large that transparency is essential. The most direct impact is for Tether to prove its innocence. The timing of this audit is crucial: regulators and market participants are demanding stronger proof of reserves from stablecoin issuers. A comprehensive audit is expected to dispel long-standing industry concerns about whether USDT has sufficient reserves and liquidity. Dr. Anya Petrova, a fintech researcher at the Global Institute for Digital Finance, points out, "For Tether, this is less about demonstrating solvency (although its audit report has aimed to do so), and more about adopting the rigorous and repeatable disclosure framework required by traditional capital markets. This could significantly reduce the risk premium perceived by institutions interacting with the USDT ecosystem." Secondly, it will set an example for compliance standards in the stablecoin industry. Circle and others will face greater competitive pressure and may also undergo similar audits, thereby raising the overall compliance level of the sector. Thirdly, a compliant Tether will further attract institutional attention. After the audit, traditional financial institutions can overcome the compliance hurdles of USDT, further increasing USDT adoption and consolidating Tether's leading position in the crypto space. Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino stated, “Tether’s mission has always been to build trust through actions, not promises. Trust is built on an institution’s willingness to undergo comprehensive scrutiny. This audit represents years of effort to strengthen the system, aiming to bring Tether to the highest standards in the global financial sector. For the hundreds of millions of individuals and businesses that rely on the US dollar₮ every day, this audit is more than just a compliance review; it’s about accountability, resilience, and confidence in the infrastructure they rely on.” “By completing this comprehensive audit, Tether aims to further strengthen the global foundation of trust underpinning the USD₮ and support the company’s mission to expand the freedom people have to store, send, and use their funds.” This audit is not only a turning point for Tether itself but may also herald a new phase for the stablecoin industry: “audit and regulation driven.”

data.title: OpenAI puts the car in reverse; 1-year-old Sora is euthanized.
data.description:
Author: Xiaojing, Tencent Technology

On March 24, 2026, OpenAI announced the shutdown of Sora.

This was OpenAI's first standalone application after ChatGPT, and it had only been officially launched for six months. On the same day, Disney announced the termination of its $1 billion investment and character licensing agreement with OpenAI, as the two parties had never completed the financial transaction.

According to foreign media reports, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman told employees at an internal meeting that shutting down Sora was to "free up resources for the next generation of AI models." Altman also announced that he would shift his personal focus to fundraising and building computing infrastructure. OpenAI's new model, codenamed "Spud," has completed initial development.

Sora was not a failed product.

Sora was not a failed product.

... Launched in September 2025, Sora surpassed one million downloads within ten days, briefly topping the App Store's free charts—a speed that even surpassed ChatGPT. However, according to mobile analytics firm Appfigures, Sora's downloads peaked at approximately 3.33 million in November 2025 before rapidly declining, dropping to around 1.13 million by February 2026. Throughout its entire lifecycle, in-app purchase revenue amounted to only about $2.1 million. Shutting down Sora itself isn't the most important thing. The important thing is that it marks OpenAI's formal admission that its "do everything" strategy of the past year has failed. And this admission of error didn't come early.

Too many "copycat tasks," the main storyline is lost

2025 was the year of the most expansion for OpenAI's product line. Video generation model Sora, AI browser Atlas, hardware devices developed in collaboration with Jony Ive, e-commerce functionality in ChatGPT—Altman once likened this strategy to "betting on a series of startups within OpenAI."

This logic was reasonable at the time.

With ChatGPT boasting 900 million weekly active users, OpenAI seems poised to launch attacks in multiple directions simultaneously, solidifying its position as a "definitive AI era" leader. However, the internal reality is quite different. Several current and former employees report that **too many parallel projects have led to a blurred strategic direction, with computing resources frequently being reallocated between teams.** The organizational structure is also becoming increasingly chaotic; **for example, the Sora team is placed under the research department, but it is actually responsible for one of the company's most anticipated products.** On March 16, 2026, Applications CEO Fidji Simo defined a new direction at an all-hands meeting. Foreign media reported on the meeting minutes, in which Simo said, "We cannot miss this moment because we are distracted by side quests." She further stated, "We really have to nail productivity in general and particularly productivity on the business front." Simo described Anthropic's rise as a "wake-up call," and said the company was "very much acting as if it's a code red." While shutting down Sora, OpenAI's new strategy has only two keywords: code and enterprise. OpenAI is merging ChatGPT, the programming tool Codex, and the browser Atlas into a single desktop "super app." Sora's video generation capabilities will no longer exist in any independent form, and the team will shift towards long-term research in robotics and world simulation. OpenAI's strategic shift is directly attributable to Anthropic's rapid rise in the coding and enterprise markets. According to data from fintech company Ramp, as of February 2026, Anthropic accounted for approximately 73% of spending among enterprises making their first purchase of AI tools, compared to OpenAI's approximately 27%. In the overall enterprise AI subscription market, OpenAI still leads with 34.4% to 24.4%, but Anthropic's month-over-month growth rate far exceeds OpenAI's. In February 2026, Anthropic grew by 4.9%, while OpenAI declined by 1.5% in the same month—OpenAI's largest single-month drop since Ramp began tracking enterprise AI purchases. Ramp economist Ara Kharazian points out that a year ago, only one in twenty-five companies paid for Anthropic; now that figure is one in a quarter. [Image: Growth curves of the two companies since they each reached $1 billion in annualized revenue; Anthropic's annual growth is approximately 10 times vs. OpenAI's approximately 3.4 times.] The gap is even more significant in the highest-value sub-segment, Coding. According to a Menlo Ventures report, Anthropic's Claude Code holds approximately 54% of the programming market share, while OpenAI holds only about 21%. After its public release in May 2025, Claude Code reached $1 billion in annualized revenue in just six months, and by February 2026, it had exceeded $2.5 billion. 70%-90% of the code produced by Anthropic's own engineering team is generated using Claude Code, and Boris Cherny, the head of Claude Code, stated that 100% of his daily coding is done using this tool.

Chart: Monthly Trend of Enterprise AI Procurement Rate -- Changes in Enterprise Subscription Procurement Rates of OpenAI (approx. 34.4%) vs. Anthropic (approx. 24.4%), with OpenAI's curve trend turning downwards

This is not just a success of "programming". Anthropic released Cowork in January 2026, a desktop agent tool for non-developers that can handle spreadsheets, file management, and report generation. ...p>

Four engineers completed the development in ten days, with most of the code written by Claude Code itself. After Cowork's release, the global SaaS sector saw a loss of approximately $2 trillion in market capitalization. In contrast, Anthropic's strategy has always been focused: no audio, no images, no video, only text, code, and enterprise scenarios. It's completely different from OpenAI, betting all its limited computing power on coding, which has proven to be the area with the highest return on investment. It's not just the product; the people, the architecture, and the North Star may all be at fault. OpenAI's deeper challenges go far beyond its multi-pronged product strategy. Since 2024, OpenAI has experienced an unprecedented loss of core talent. Co-founder and Chief Scientist Ilya Sutskever left OpenAI in May 2024 to found Safe Superintelligence (SSI), which was valued at over $30 billion in less than a year. Jan Leike, co-head of the Superintelligence team, resigned around the same time, publicly criticizing OpenAI's security culture as "giving way to glamorous products," and subsequently joined Anthropic. CTO Mira Murati left in September 2024 to found Thinking Machines Lab, while Chief Research Officer Bob McGrew and VP of Research Barret Zoph left on the same day. Co-founder John Schulman first went to Anthropic before joining Murati's company. According to the Seoul Economic Daily, over 50 researchers and engineers have left for competitors such as Anthropic and Meta in the past year. By early 2026, of OpenAI's original 11 co-founders, only Altman and President Greg Brockman remain with the company. These individuals were all pillars of OpenAI in certain areas. Sutskever was the driving force behind OpenAI's technical direction, leading core research breakthroughs from the GPT series to inference models (such as O1). His departure likely means the company has lost the most insightful person on the fundamental question of "which direction the model should evolve in." Murati, on the other hand, was the key figure in translating research results into products; her role was irreplaceable for the launch of ChatGPT. When a company simultaneously loses its technological leader and product hub, who can make the right decisions? Judging from recent photos, Sam Altman's hair has turned completely white.
