The tug-of-war between capital, IPO, and strategy: OpenAI's current situation is compounded by a unique pressure: it may become the largest unlisted company in human history, and its cash burn rate is astonishing. In February 2026, OpenAI completed an $110 billion funding round, valuing the company at $730 billion pre-money and approximately $840 billion post-money, making it the largest single-round private equity funding in history. Amazon invested $50 billion, while Nvidia and SoftBank each invested $30 billion. However, these figures come with numerous conditions: Amazon's $35 billion is contingent on OpenAI achieving its AGI target or going public. In 2025, OpenAI's revenue was approximately $13.1 billion, but it incurred a loss of approximately $8 billion during the same period. According to foreign media reports, losses are projected to soar to $25 billion in 2026, and cumulative losses could reach $115 billion from now until 2029. OpenAI expects to become profitable no earlier than the 2030s. Its cash burn rate in 2026 is estimated at approximately 83.3%, an extremely rare occurrence in startup history. This capital structure forms a delicate but fragile closed loop: Nvidia is both an investor and a GPU supplier, Amazon and Microsoft are both investors and cloud service providers, and SoftBank is a partner in the Stargate data center project. A significant portion of the funding will flow back to investors in the form of service fees. Altman acknowledged the validity of this concern in a CNBC interview, but stated that "it only makes sense if new revenue flows into the entire AI ecosystem." Under this capital structure, an IPO is an unavoidable choice. OpenAI aims to go public by the end of 2026. Before going public, it must present a clear growth story to public market investors. This is the direct driving force behind the strategic shift of "cutting redundancy and focusing on productivity." Meanwhile, Anthropic's funding is equally impressive, completing a $30 billion Series G round in February 2026 with a valuation of $38 billion. However, its revenue structure appears healthier: approximately 80% comes from enterprise clients, with annualized revenue reaching about $14 billion, maintaining roughly tenfold growth for three consecutive years. Research firm Epoch AI predicts that if the current growth trend continues, Anthropic's annual revenue could surpass OpenAI's in the second half of 2026. The giants haven't fallen yet. The competition is far from over; OpenAI still possesses significant structural advantages: over 800 million weekly active users, the highest consumer AI brand awareness globally, and a newly acquired $110 billion in funding. ChatGPT's consumer subscription revenue continues to grow, its enterprise clients have exceeded 1 million, and its new model, Spud, is about to be released. Codex programming tools already have over 2 million weekly active users. However, the AI ​​industry is at a critical juncture of paradigm shift, with the focus of value moving from "whose model is the strongest" to "who can help companies improve productivity," and from a competition of general capabilities to Agentic AI—AI systems capable of autonomously completing complex tasks. In this paradigm shift, Anthropic bet on the right direction, while OpenAI bet everything, but essentially bet nothing. The shutdown of Sora is a signal that OpenAI's management has recognized the problem. However, there is still a long way to go between recognizing the problem and solving it. With the founding team almost entirely gone, unprecedented capital obligations, and competitors already ahead, can OpenAI return to its original form, maintain its initial goals, and make the right choices? From a laboratory to the world's most valuable unlisted company, OpenAI took less than four years. The next four quarters will determine whether it can transform from a "big" company into a "right" company, or, in extreme cases, a company heading towards decline.

data.title: Some insights and reflections on Openclaw shrimp farming
data.description:
Author: Haotian; Source: X, @tmel0211

As a "shrimp farmer," after several weeks of painful struggle, I'd like to share some insights and experiences on shrimp farming for your reference only::

1) Everyone knows that shrimp farming is for "efficiency improvement," but the truth is that at this stage, most ordinary people are "wasting their time" in shrimp farming. There are many unexpected troubles, such as Claude's account being banned, API forwarding quotas being blocked, and Openclaw suddenly upgrading and losing its "memory," etc. This will consume most of the time, and the efficiency gains brought are far from proportional; 2) I saw various posts on Twitter timelines selling AI anxiety. Just take a look and move on. Such as how a single command can make Claude control your entire computer, or how a single prompt will allow AI to permanently take over your work and make you lie down, blah blah blah. The reality is, not only is it difficult to work comfortably, but people often code until the wee hours of the morning for a single feature, frequently fixing one bug only to have another pop up. It's hard to imagine how those who aren't even willing to actually operate the system for five minutes can so confidently proclaim the arrival of AGI and that AI will revolutionize everything; 3) Raising lobsters can indeed fulfill many people's OPC (One Person Company) dreams, but while the upper limit of a large model's capabilities is equal for everyone, the cognitive level of different people in managing large models varies drastically. Don't assume everyone can become Peter Steinberger, Matt Schlicht, or Andrej Karpathy. The thinking, framework design, iteration experience, ability level, and delivery results of a brilliant developer and an ordinary person in their so-called lobster-raising can be worlds apart; 4) Raising lobsters essentially builds your own personalized AI. The OS, theoretically, involves multiple parallel connections between large models, managing the number of digital employees, the levels of proactive and reactive collaboration, the friction of nested skills and combined collaborative task delivery, etc. The more overlapping requirements of Cron, jobs, real-time data scanning, and trading skills, the greater the probability of conflict and the greater the optimization challenge. It's important to understand that shrimp farming is never a "model capability" problem, but rather an "engineering implementation optimization" problem. 5) Configuring all digital employees with Opus 4.6 and Gemini 2.5 Flash lite might both accomplish the same task, but the former is like hiring seasoned Wall Street elites, while the latter is more like hiring slaves from the slums. They might both achieve the same function, but the cost and the delivered result are incomparable. The truth is, the time you spend painstakingly debugging might be easily replaced by someone else simply by having the financial means. Shrimp farming is incredibly expensive. What's frightening is that most people understand this, yet they still compromise and constantly optimize with subpar models. (6) Managing digital employees is like stacking Lego bricks: the more employees, the more skills, and the more complex the job scenarios, the higher the probability of sudden collapse.) For some tasks, just be capable enough; don't be insatiable and impose "requirements" beyond your cognitive boundaries. It's highly recommended to invest more effort in memory engineering, Git version control, and dispelling model illusions; otherwise, there will be many moments that will push you to the brink of collapse. One second you might be feeling great about completing a cool requirement, and the next second everything will have collapsed, leaving you heartbroken. Don't ask me how I know. (That's all.)
